lesson.title: Grammar Essentials
lesson.explanation: Grammar is the structure of language — the rules that make communication clear. Nouns name things (person, place, idea). Verbs show action or state (run, is, think). Adjectives describe nouns (big, red, clever). Adverbs modify verbs (quickly, very, always). Prepositions show relationships (in, on, between). Tenses locate actions in time: past (walked), present (walk), future (will walk). Subject-verb agreement ensures clarity: 'She runs' not 'She run'. Master these and your writing becomes instantly clearer.

practice_problems.question: Identify the verb in: 'The dog chased the cat across the garden.'
practice_problems.explanation: 'Chased' is the action word — what the dog did.

practice_problems.question: Choose the correct form: 'The children (is/are) playing outside.'
practice_problems.answer: are — 'children' is plural, so the verb must be plural too
practice_problems.explanation: Subject-verb agreement: plural subject needs plural verb.

practice_problems.question: Change to past tense: 'She writes a letter every day.'
practice_problems.answer: She wrote a letter every day.
hints: What is the past tense of 'writes'?
practice_problems.explanation: write → wrote (irregular verb). 'Every day' stays the same.

practice_problems.question: Identify the adjective and the noun it describes: 'The tall building collapsed during the earthquake.'
hints: Which word describes a quality of the building?
practice_problems.explanation: Adjectives come before the noun they modify.

practice_problems.question: Rewrite using active voice: 'The cake was eaten by the children.'
hints: Who did the action? Put them first.
practice_problems.explanation: Active: subject does action. Passive: subject receives action. Active is usually clearer.
distractors: Eating was done by children to cake

misconceptions.misconception: Grammar Essentials rules are just memorisation
misconceptions.correction: Understanding WHY rules work helps you apply them in new situations.
